Tafseer:
1. Word Analysis and Explanation of the Verses
(O you who have believed, do not take My enemies and your enemies as allies, extending to them affection while they have disbelieved in what came to you of the truth, having driven out the Messenger and yourselves [only] because you believe in Allah, your Lord. If you have come out for jihad in My cause and seeking My pleasure, you confide in them affection, while I am most knowing of what you have concealed and what you have declared. And whoever does it among you has certainly strayed from the straight path) (1).
(Warning to opportunistic Muslims): Although the address is general in terms of wording, it is directed toward those Muslims who, after passing through the stage of migration, still maintained consideration for their former family and close relations with the polytheists of Mecca. As long as a general battle against the polytheists was not commanded, this weakness of theirs remained hidden; however, when the command for their general pursuit was given, this weakness began to show. These people hesitated to raise their swords against their family and tribe, and they also wished to keep the leaders of the Quraysh pleased so that they would not harm their relatives. Although this approach was based more on opportunism than hypocrisy, they assumed that if they maintained good relations with their polytheist relatives, the latter would be influenced by their behavior and accept Islam one day. However, the Qur'an declared this opportunism incompatible with faith and made it clear to them that the faith they claim requires them not to maintain relations of alliance with those who are both Allah's enemies and theirs.
The word 'aduww' (enemy) is used for both singular and plural. It is on the scale of 'fa'ul', and in Arabic, this scale is identical for both, and does not even distinguish between masculine and feminine. (you extend to them affection while they have disbelieved in what came to you of the truth, having driven out the Messenger and yourselves because you believe in Allah, your Lord) - This is an explanation of the preceding statement: you extend friendship and love to them, while the reality is that they are both Allah's enemies and yours. Their enmity is clear from the fact that they rejected the religion that came to you, and they intend to exile the Messenger and you for the crime of believing in Allah, Who is your Lord.
(you extend to them affection) is a style of expression similar to (And do not throw yourselves with your own hands into destruction) (Al-Baqarah, 2:195). Such matters are carried out through letters, messages, and various means, making this style of expression highly suitable for conveying the meaning. We have translated it as 'extending affection' to preserve the significance of this style.
In (having driven out the Messenger and yourselves), the present tense is used to vividly depict the situation before their eyes, in order to evoke a sense of self-respect in those who wished to maintain relations with such heartless people who exiled the Messenger and his companions from their homeland. It means: If, despite this, you still extend friendship to them, you should evaluate your faith and Islam, for their entire anger is solely because you believed in Allah, your Lord! 'Your Lord' (Rabbikum) serves as a proof here that since Allah is the Lord, He alone is entitled to faith. If you believed in Him, you recognized the true Right-holder, but this very righteousness of yours has become the cause of their wrath.
Regarding the structure of (because you believe - an tu'minu), we have been explaining in various places that sometimes a construct noun (mudhaf) is omitted before 'an'. If we unpack it, the meaning is: they are expelling you for this charge or sin, namely, why did you believe in Allah, your Lord? It is as if your greatest virtue and righteousness have become, in their eyes, your greatest crime. (If you have come out for jihad in My cause and seeking My pleasure, you confide in them affection, while I am most knowing of what you have concealed and what you have declared. And whoever does it among you has certainly strayed from the straight path).
(Explanation of a grammatical structure): This portion's grammatical composition was not fully clear to many commentators, which is why they could not explain its meaning clearly. Let us first understand its grammatical construction, then we will try to explain its meaning. Commentators generally assume that the response (jawab) to the conditional clause is omitted here and have tried to determine it through context. However, in my view, the response to both conditions—(in kuntum - if you) and (man yaf'alhu - whoever does it)—is the same, namely: (has certainly strayed from the straight path - faqad dhalla sawa'as-sabil). 'Tusirruna' (you confide) is a circumstantial clause derived from the pronoun of address, and (while I am most knowing of what you have concealed and what you have declared) is a parenthetical sentence. The meaning is: If you have come out to strive in My cause and to seek My pleasure, while in this state you harbor a desire for alliance with the enemies of Allah and His Messenger in your hearts—even though I know both what you hide and what you declare—then remember that whoever among you does this has indeed strayed from the straight path.
(Seeking Allah's pleasure and allying with His enemies cannot coexist): The reason for straying from the straight path is obvious: striving in the cause of Allah to seek His pleasure, and befriending the enemies of Allah and His Messenger, are two completely contradictory things. They cannot coexist in a person's heart at the same time. If Allah were not aware of both the hidden and the manifest, He could perhaps be deceived; but when He is fully aware of what is hidden and manifest in everyone, how can He be deceived? Those who go out carrying such contradictory desires in their hearts must remember that they have set out not in the cause of God, but in the path of Satan.
'This path you are treading leads to Turkestan' [i.e., the wrong direction].
(A point regarding the context of revelation): Under this verse, commentators have narrated the incident of Hatib bin Abi Balta'ah (may Allah be pleased with him) as the context of revelation (shan-i nuzul). Regarding the context of revelation, we have already quoted the opinion of our master and teacher (may Allah have mercy on him) in the introduction to the book, which is that when the predecessors narrate an event as the context of revelation for a verse, it does not necessarily mean that that exact event was the sole cause for the revelation of the verse. Rather, their purpose is to show that this verse contains the ruling for such events. If you reflect on this verse, you will see that it does not point to a specific incident but to a specific type of situation. It is not surprising that the weakness pointed out in the verse was found in some people; rather, it is the result of common human weakness, or, as we indicated, it could even be based on a perceived good interest, and this aspect is more reasonable here, as will become clear from the subsequent verses. But the Knower of the true interests is Allah, the All-Knowing and All-Wise. Often, a person forms an opinion with good intentions, but some weakness of their self may be hidden within it, which their vision fails to reach. Here, Allah Almighty has warned against such weaknesses so that every action of the vanguard of this Ummah serves as a model for those who come after.

(If they gain dominance over you, they would be to you as enemies and extend against you their hands and their tongues with evil, and they wish you would disbelieve) (2).
(The state of the disbelievers' animosity): That is, you wish for friendship with them, but their hearts are so filled with animosity against you that if they were to gain dominance over you anywhere, they would neither refrain from physical assault nor verbal abuse; rather, their ultimate effort would be to turn you into apostates. This same point is described in Surah At-Tawbah as: (And if they gain dominance over you, they do not observe concerning you any pact or treaty) (At-Tawbah, 9:8). It means that their enmity toward you is collective, and they are so severe in it that they will not respect any relation, kinship, covenant, or treaty. Therefore, do not expect any goodness from them; instead, the correct attitude for you is to abandon any desire for alliance with them.

(Neither your relatives nor your children will benefit you on the Day of Resurrection; He will judge between you. And Allah is Seeing of what you do) (3).
(Kinship will not avail on the Day of Resurrection): 'Arham' here is used in the sense of relations and family ties. This is a warning that those who prioritize their family relations over religious obligations must remember that these things will not avail them on the Day of Resurrection. On that Day, Allah Almighty will separate all such relatives from one another. The depiction of that Day is drawn as follows:
(And no friend will ask of a friend, though they will be made to see each other. The criminal will wish that he could be ransomed from the punishment of that Day by his children, and his wife and his brother, and his nearest kindred who sheltered him, and whoever is on earth entirely, then it would save him) (Al-Ma'arij, 70:10-14).
This same truth is explained in Surah 'Abasa as:
(The Day a man will flee from his brother, and his mother and his father, and his wife and his children) (Abasa, 80:34-36).
Addressing similar Muslims, this warning was given in Surah At-Tawbah as:
(O you who have believed, do not take your fathers and your brothers as allies if they have preferred disbelief over belief. And whoever among you allies with them - then those are the wrongdoers. Say, [O Muhammad], 'If your fathers, your sons, your brothers, your wives, your relatives, wealth which you have obtained, commerce wherein you fear decline, and dwellings with which you are pleased are more beloved to you than Allah and His Messenger and jihad in His cause, then wait until Allah delivers His command. And Allah does not guide the defiantly disobedient people.') (At-Tawbah, 9:23-24).
In the verse under discussion, (on the Day of Resurrection) is placed beautifully in the middle, serving as the adverb of time for both the preceding and succeeding verbs. (And Allah is Seeing of what you do) - This is another warning: do not remain under the delusion that what you are trying to do in secret will remain hidden from Allah. Nothing remains hidden from Allah. Every action of yours is in His sight.

(There has already been for you an excellent pattern in Abraham and those with him, when they said to their people, 'Indeed, we are disassociated from you and from whatever you worship other than Allah. We have denied you, and there has appeared between us and you animosity and hatred forever until you believe in Allah alone'—except for the saying of Abraham to his father, 'I will surely ask forgiveness for you, but I have not [power to do] for you against Allah anything. Our Lord, upon You we have relied, and to You we have returned, and to You is the destination') (4).
(Exhortation to follow the pattern of Abraham): It is stated that you do not need to look far to find guidance in this matter. An excellent, exemplary model already exists for you in the life of your forefather Abraham (peace be upon him) and his companions. The phrasing of 'has already been' (qad kanat) suggests that this is not something hidden or unknown, but is already before you. It is well known that the Arabs took pride in being the descendants of Abraham and Ishmael (peace be upon them), and they were familiar with the history of their migration and sacrifice. Although layers of dust had gathered over these narratives through the passage of time and some aspects had been distorted by innovations, they were by no means entirely ignorant of them, as some historians have assumed. The phrase 'and those with him' indicates that when Abraham (peace be upon him) migrated, he did not do so alone; rather, those of his people who believed in him accompanied him in this migration.
(Abraham's declaration of disassociation): 'when they said to their people' - This refers to the declaration of disassociation that Abraham (peace be upon him) and his companions made to their people. They openly declared that they were completely free of them and all the idols they worshipped besides Allah. They rejected their creed, and with this declaration of disassociation, enmity and hatred between them became manifest until they should believe in Allah alone.
(And there has appeared between us and you animosity): That is, until now, we showed you consideration to convey the call of religion to you, but now that the proof has been established against you and you persist in your deviation, we disassociate from you and separate ourselves. From now on, our relationship with you will be one of open animosity, unless you repent of your shirk and accept the call of monotheism. 'except for the saying of Abraham to his father' - Commentators have generally understood this as an exception to the 'excellent pattern', but in our view, it is an exception to the declaration of disassociation. That is, in this open declaration of disassociation, if Abraham (peace be upon him) showed any consideration to his father, it was only that he promised to pray for his forgiveness from his Lord, even though he had no authority from God in his father's case. In other words: whatever Allah wills will happen, but I will still pray.
The reason for this consideration, as clarified in other parts of the Qur'an, was that Abraham was exceptionally tender-hearted and forbearing. He thought that praying for his father's forgiveness would not contradict the general disassociation from his people, but was rather a requirement of the filial duty and goodness that every son owes to his parents. At that time, he did not fully realize the extent of his father's hostility to the religion. He assumed his father's anger was merely out of a desire to save his son from what he perceived as deviation. However, once it became fully clear to him that his father was an active enemy of Allah's religion, he disassociated from him completely.
(But I have not [power to do] for you against Allah anything): Along with his promise of seeking forgiveness, Abraham (peace be upon him) clarified the core reality of monotheism: his only authority was to pray for forgiveness, while granting or denying forgiveness rested entirely with Allah. He had no say in the matter. It should be noted here that seeking forgiveness for someone is a form of intercession. When a prophet of Abraham's stature expresses such helplessness regarding intercession, what can be said of others?
(The prayer of migration): 'Our Lord, upon You we have relied, and to You we have returned, and to You is the destination' - This prayer is connected to the declaration of disassociation. The phrase 'except for the saying of Abraham' appeared in the middle as a parenthetical sentence. As soon as it ended, the prayer offered by Abraham (peace be upon him) and his companions at that critical juncture is recorded. We have explained in other places that after the declaration of disassociation, the stage of migration inevitably arrives for a prophet and his companions. This is an extremely difficult phase. Severing ties and declaring eternal enmity and disassociation from one's entire nation is no easy task. Therefore, every messenger, at the time of migration, entrusted his affairs and those of his companions to his Lord. Prophet Moses (peace be upon him) offered a similar prayer at the time of his migration, and a similar prayer is narrated from our Holy Prophet (peace and blessings of Allah be upon him). This prayer demonstrates that even a servant's best intentions are realized only through Allah's guidance; hence, every step must be taken relying on His help. In Allah's trials, only those emerge successful whose hearts remain constantly inclined toward Him and who possess the firm conviction that they must eventually return to their Lord.

(Our Lord, make us not [objects of] trial for the disbelievers, and forgive us, our Lord. Indeed, it is You Who are the Exalted in Might, the Wise) (5).
This is also part of the prayer mentioned above. 'Fitnah' here means the target of trial, referring to the persecution that the disbelievers could inflict upon the Muslims. The meaning is: Out of honor and zeal for Your monotheism, we have declared our disassociation and enmity toward these polytheists. Now, we are prepared to face whatever comes from them, but our reliance is on Your help. Do not give them so much rein that they make us the targets of their oppression. (And forgive us, our Lord) - O our Lord, forgive our weaknesses and sins, lest our sins become the reason for our enemies to find us weak and make us the targets of their tyranny. This phrase points to the reality that if any trial befalls us, it will be due to our own deeds; and since everything is in Your power, if You forgive our sins, we will be saved from their consequences.
(Indeed, it is You Who are the Exalted in Might, the Wise): This is an expression of complete submission. You are dominant over all things and can do whatever You will; no one can hold back Your hand. Along with this, You are All-Wise; Your every action is based on wisdom. Therefore, we entrust our affairs entirely to You. Whatever You do will contain goodness, wisdom, and benefit.

(There has certainly been for you in them an excellent pattern for anyone whose hope is in Allah and the Last Day. And whoever turns away—then indeed, Allah is the Free of need, the Praiseworthy) (6).
(An emphatic warning): This serves as an exchange (badal) for the phrase 'There has already been for you an excellent pattern'. The meaning is: while the example of Abraham (peace be upon him) and his companions in declaring disassociation and enmity is indeed excellent, following this pattern is not for the faint-hearted. Only those can find the courage to do so who hope for Allah's help and look forward to the Last Day. Those who do not have these two convictions firmly established in their hearts cannot play this part. The point is that leaving behind one's home, wealth, homeland, and nation is possible only for those who expect God's help at every step and whose primary reliance is not on the temporary goods of this world, but on the grace and reward of the Hereafter. This verse makes it entirely clear what the essential provision for the path of migration is, and it also reveals what lay at the root of the weaknesses shown by some people during this phase.
(And whoever turns away—then indeed, Allah is the Free of need, the Praiseworthy): This is a warning to those who turn back. The path of success lies in following the pattern of Abraham (peace be upon him) and his companions; otherwise, remember that if anyone turns away, Allah has no need of them. Allah is not dependent on anyone; rather, He is completely self-sufficient and inherently praiseworthy. His sovereignty is established on His own might, not on the support of others.

(Perhaps Allah will put, between you and those to whom you have been enemies among them, affection. And Allah is competent, and Allah is Forgiving and Merciful) (7).
(A great glad tiding): This is a very significant glad tiding: if you muster the courage to declare enmity toward your relatives today, do not think that this animosity will remain forever. It is entirely possible that Allah will change this enmity into affection, and those whom you must oppose today will accept Islam and embrace you as brothers. (And Allah is competent, and Allah is Forgiving and Merciful) - Allah is powerful over all things. If He wills, He can turn bitter enemies into close friends. Furthermore, He is Forgiving and Merciful; He does not look for excuses to punish people, but rather looks for ways to bless them with forgiveness and mercy. One should not assume that His staunchest enemies will always remain so; perhaps Allah's mercy will open the way of guidance for them as well. The reference to the attributes 'Forgiving and Merciful' also contains a glad tiding for those who had maintained secret ties with the enemies of Allah: if they take heed after this warning and reform their behavior, Allah is Forgiving and Merciful and will forgive their past weaknesses.
(A psychological aspect of migration): The glad tiding regarding the people of Mecca accepting Islam has a specific psychological reason that is worth noting here. When they saw that many of their brothers, sisters, and close relatives were leaving behind their homes, families, and everything they owned solely for the sake of religion—even though these individuals were the finest among them—they began to realize that opposing this call with oppression and violence was wrong, and that they needed to review their own attitude. I have mentioned elsewhere that what first inclined a great personality like Umar (may Allah be pleased with him) toward Islam was seeing the migration of some oppressed men and women to Abyssinia. This migration was bound to affect every sensitive person, and indeed, the pace of accepting Islam accelerated greatly after the migration. To allow this natural process to continue, it was crucial that no group among the emigrants showed weakness to the people of Mecca. If they had shown any weakness, the Meccans would have assumed that the Muslims had migrated but now regretted their action and wished to re-establish friendly relations. This would have suppressed any emerging inclination toward Islam among the Meccans and demoralized the vulnerable Muslims still trapped in Mecca. Therefore, the Qur'an strictly corrected this weakness and warned the people not to cultivate friendly ties with the enemies of religion. It was made clear that embracing those steeped in disbelief would lead to ruin. However, if they stood firm in their opposition, Allah would eventually guide them to Islam and make them friends. Indeed, Allah fulfilled this promise, and at the time of the Conquest of Mecca, the people witnessed the scene of 'entering the religion of Allah in multitudes' (An-Nasr, 110:2).

2. Subject Matter of Verses 8-9
Next, the Qur'an clarifies that the prohibition is against maintaining alliances and close friendship with those who have expelled Muslims from their homes or assisted in their expulsion. It does not forbid showing kindness and justice to those who have not committed such transgressions. Some believe these verses were revealed later, when the Muslims' attitude toward the disbelievers became overly severe in implementing the previous command. However, there is no need to assume such a timeline. As established, the address in the preceding verses was to those who wished to maintain ties with the Meccans. Both points could be taught together: that befriending active enemies of the religion is incompatible with the faith they claim, but showing kindness and justice to those whose attitude is not aggressive is highly appropriate and fitting for them. Read the verses.
3. Word Analysis and Explanation of the Verses
(Allah does not forbid you from those who do not fight you because of religion and do not expel you from your homes - from being righteous toward them and acting justly toward them. Indeed, Allah loves those who act justly) (8).
(Defining the boundaries of the prohibition): The analysis of the word 'birr' (righteousness/kindness) was presented in the commentary of Surah Al-Baqarah, verse 44. It refers to maintaining family ties, doing good, and fulfilling rights. 'Iqsat' means acting with justice and equity, ensuring that everyone receives their due right without any shortfall. It is stated: The command given to you, 'do not take My enemies and your enemies as allies' (verse 1), does not mean you should not show kindness and justice to those disbelievers who neither fought you over religion nor expelled you from your homes. The prohibited action, as clarified in the next verse, is taking them as allies, not showing them justice and kindness. Moreover, this prohibition does not apply to all disbelievers, but only to those who actively fought you over religion and exiled you.
The restriction 'because of religion' shows that the discussion here is not about ordinary disputes arising from conflicting family or national interests, but specifically about the warfare initiated by the disbelievers solely out of opposition to religion, with the aim of preventing people from worshipping Allah alone. Since religion is the shared treasure of all believers and their salvation depends on it, any Muslim who allies with the enemies of religion is untruthful in their claim of faith.
(A question and its answer): (Indeed, Allah loves those who act justly) - This encourages those who practice justice. A question arises here: since both 'kindness' (birr) and 'justice' (qist) were mentioned, why does the verse specifically highlight Allah's love for those who practice justice? In my view, the answer is that acts of kindness, such as maintaining family ties, are not as difficult for the human soul as practicing justice, especially when dealing with disbelievers. Supporting the weak, helping the needy, or maintaining relations with disbelieving parents are not exceptionally difficult tasks, as strong natural human instincts support them. However, fulfilling the rights of justice, particularly when dealing with enemies, is no easy task. Therefore, the Qur'an accords a special status of divine love to those who meet this challenge. It should be noted that establishing justice and equity is one of the core purposes of this Ummah's mission. Those who practice equal justice with both friend and foe represent the finest of this Ummah and are beloved to Allah. Without fulfilling this duty of justice, other good deeds remain largely ineffective.

(Allah only forbids you from those who fight you because of religion and expel you from your homes and support your expulsion - [forbids] that you make allies of them. And whoever makes allies of them, then it is those who are the wrongdoers) (9).
(Who are prohibited, and what is prohibited?): This explicitly states whom Allah forbids you from allying with, and what exactly is forbidden. It is stated: He forbids you from those who fought you over religion, expelled you from your homes, or assisted your enemies in expelling you. The prohibited act is taking them as allies (tawalli). Taking them as allies means disregarding the collective interests of the Muslim community to offer them cooperation in order to serve some personal interest.
If you reflect on this verse, you will see that the emphasis of the restriction is on the phrase 'that you make allies of them' (an tawallawhum). What is forbidden is 'tawalli'—taking these disbelievers as friends and protectors—not showing them kindness and justice. Kindness is a unilateral act. It does not depend on the attitude of the recipient. If someone is in need, it is our moral duty to help them, whether they are a disbeliever or a Muslim, without expecting any thanks or reward from them: (We wish not from you reward or gratitude) (Ad-Dahr, 76:9). Even if we harbor enmity toward someone for some reason, the correct attitude is still to treat them with kindness. As clarified in the Qur'an and Hadith, such kindness yields a greater reward.
As for justice and equity, they are based on law, covenants, and recognized norms. In this, there is no distinction between disbeliever and believer, or friend and foe. Whatever the law and covenant require must be fulfilled, regardless of whether the party involved is a friend or an enemy. In the subsequent verses, decisions regarding certain disputes with the polytheists of Quraysh are presented, illustrating how the Qur'an rendered impartial judgments and urged Muslims to act upon them. (And whoever makes allies of them, then it is those who are the wrongdoers) - This means that if any Muslim still maintains alliances with these disbelievers after this warning, they should know that they will not harm Allah or Islam in the least; rather, they will only wrong their own souls.

4. Subject Matter of Verses 10-13
Next, in the light of the instruction 'do not take My enemies and your enemies as allies' mentioned in the first verse, Muslims are first instructed regarding the women who migrate from Mecca. Their faith and commitment to Islam must be verified; they should not be accepted into the community simply because they have arrived and wish to join. If verification shows that their migration was indeed for the sake of religion, they should be accepted; otherwise, they should be returned. Similarly, those emigrants whose wives remained in Mecca as disbelievers are not permitted to maintain their marriage bonds with them; instead, they must release them so they may marry whom they wish, and their dowers (mahr) should be settled between the parties.
The second instruction is given to the Prophet (peace and blessings of Allah be upon him): when women come to pledge allegiance (Bay'ah), he should take their pledge upon their commitment to practice all the recognized virtues of Islamic life. If they pledge to undertake this responsibility, their allegiance should be accepted; otherwise, it should not. The Islamic society is not a place for indiscriminate recruitment; eligibility to join belongs only to those who pledge to fulfill the requirements of faith and Islam.
(Resolution of an important dispute regarding the Treaty of Hudaybiyyah): These instructions are primarily intended to demonstrate that a union between Islam and non-Islam is contrary to reason and nature, and this must be scrutinized in every aspect of relationships so that no trace of hypocrisy remains. Just as it is impermissible for Muslims to maintain affection for the opponents of Allah and His Messenger, it is also impermissible for them to maintain marriage ties with polytheist women. If such a connection has persisted, the time has come to sever it. However, this issue also required resolving a dispute that had existed between the Quraysh and the Muslims regarding the Treaty of Hudaybiyyah from its inception, which had reached a point where a decisive ruling was unavoidable.
It will be recalled that an important clause in the Treaty of Hudaybiyyah stipulated that if anyone from the Quraysh came to the Muslims, even if they had accepted Islam, the Muslims were bound to return them; conversely, if a Muslim went to the Quraysh, they were not bound to return him. Although there are differences among narrators regarding the exact wording of this clause, the narration from Urwah, Dahhak, Abdur Rahman bin Zayd, Zuhri, Muqatil bin Hayyan, and Suddi records the words as: (on the condition that no one from us comes to you, even if he is upon your religion, except that you must return him to us). This meant that any person from the Quraysh, even if sharing the faith of Muhammad (peace and blessings of Allah be upon him), had to be returned if they went to him.
The Muslims accepted this clause with respect to men; consequently, Abu Jandal (may Allah be pleased with him) was returned under highly emotional circumstances at the very moment the treaty was being finalized. However, with respect to women, the Muslims did not accept that these words applied to them, and anyone familiar with the Arabic language would agree that the words are not explicit regarding the return of women. Although the word 'ahad' (anyone/no one) used in the treaty carries a general meaning, a close examination reveals that all subsequent pronouns and verbs are masculine. In such a case, one might argue that women are included in the general meaning of 'ahad', but no reasonable person can claim that the wording is definitive for women in the same way it is for men. On the contrary, it can be argued that if the parties had intended for this clause to definitively cover women, an explicit clarification such as 'whether male or female' would have been added. Since no such clarification was included, despite the meticulous nature of the treaty, it clearly indicates that the issue of women was not in the minds of the parties at the time of the agreement. This ambiguity naturally led to a dispute between the Muslims and the Quraysh, which became highly significant around the time of this Surah's revelation, as several women migrated to Medina and the Muslims needed a definitive policy. Consequently, Allah Almighty issued a decisive ruling, clarifying: (That is the judgment of Allah; He judges between you, and Allah is Knowing and Wise). Read the verses in this light.
5. Word Analysis and Explanation of the Verses
(O you who have believed, when the believing women come to you as emigrants, examine them. Allah is most knowing as to their faith. And if you know them to be believers, then do not return them to the disbelievers; they are not lawful [wives] for them, nor are they lawful [husbands] for them. And give the disbelievers what they have spent. And there is no blame upon you if you marry them when you have given them their dowers. And hold not to marriage bonds with disbelieving women, and ask for what you have spent and let them ask for what they have spent. That is the judgment of Allah; He judges between you. And Allah is Knowing and Wise) (10).
(Instruction to verify the faith of migrating women): The Muslims are instructed that they are not obliged to return Muslim women who migrate to them from the land of disbelief, as noted in the introductory discussion. However, it is also impermissible to simply admit them into their homes without verifying their faith. It is necessary to examine the women who arrive to ensure that their migration was truly for the sake of Islam and not motivated by some other worldly reason. If verification brings reassurance that their migration was indeed for Islam, then they must not be returned; but if they cannot provide this reassurance, they should not be retained, for the Islamic society is a society of pure men and women, not of the impure. The nature of this examination is clarified in a narration from Ibn Abbas (may Allah be pleased with him):
(Ibn Abbas was asked how the Messenger of Allah examined the women. He replied: He would make them swear by Allah that they did not leave out of hatred for their husbands, and swear by Allah that they did not leave out of a desire to move from one land to another, and swear by Allah that they did not leave seeking worldly gain, and swear by Allah that they only left out of love for Allah and His Messenger).
(Allah is most knowing as to their faith): This is a parenthetical phrase. The meaning is: try to verify their state to the best of your ability using oaths and circumstantial evidence. As for the absolute reality, it is known only to Allah. If, despite your best efforts, you do not reach the absolute truth, you are excused before Allah; and if they have deceived you, they should know that Allah is fully aware of their faith or disbelief. (And if you know them to be believers, then do not return them to the disbelievers) - If the available means of verification establish that they are believers, do not hand them over to the disbelievers, because they are not lawful for the disbelievers, nor are the disbelievers lawful for them; rather, they are forbidden to each other.
(A just settlement): With this command, the Qur'an resolved the dispute regarding the clause of the Treaty of Hudaybiyyah, and a close examination shows that this decision is entirely just. Even if the Qur'an had ruled that the Muslims were not bound to return any women under the treaty, it would not have been unjustified, as it would simply have been taking advantage of the literal wording. However, the Qur'an did not merely exploit the wording but made a decision based on a solid intellectual and moral foundation. It commanded the return of those women whose migration was not for the sake of Allah and His Messenger but for some worldly purpose, while prohibiting the return of those whose faith was established. This is a principled decision that no reasonable person can dispute. Human beings possess an intellectual and moral existence, and forcing someone to remain bound to a society where their moral and intellectual identity is not secure is a clear injustice. Women, being the vulnerable gender, are particularly deserving of protection. (And give the disbelievers what they have spent) - This is another just instruction: if a believing woman is retained and she was married to a disbeliever, the Muslims are responsible for returning the dower (mahr) her husband had paid her. Practically, this repayment would be handled through the public treasury (Bayt al-Mal). Although the words 'what they have spent' are general, the dower is the primary matter under discussion in the event of a marital separation, so context dictates that this is what is meant.
(And there is no blame upon you if you marry them when you have given them their dowers): After these stages are settled, any Muslim who wishes to marry them may do so, provided he pays them their dower. This means that in addition to the dower returned to the former husband, the new husband must pay a dower to the woman herself. The phrase 'there is no blame' is used to show that once a woman has entered the land of Islam and joined the Muslim society, her previous marriage in the land of disbelief, or the absence of her non-Muslim parents' or guardians' permission, cannot stand as an obstacle to her marriage. She is now free from all restrictions of her disbelieving husband and relatives, and within the limits of Islamic Shariah, she is her own master.
(Another generous step): (And hold not to marriage bonds with disbelieving women) - 'Isam' is the plural of 'ismah' (bond). In this regard, Islam took another generous and entirely unilateral step by instructing Muslims: if your wives are in the land of disbelief and persist in their disbelief, do not maintain your marriage bonds with them; instead, release them from the marriage tie so they may marry whom they wish. (and ask for what you have spent and let them ask for what they have spent) - Following this declaration, the practical arrangement was that the marriage of women who accepted Islam and joined the Muslims was terminated with their disbelieving husbands, and the marriage of women who were married to Muslims but chose to remain in the land of disbelief was annulled. As for their dowers, they should be settled collectively: the dowers that Muslims paid to their disbelieving wives should be returned by the disbelievers to the Muslims, and the dowers that the disbelievers paid to their wives who accepted Islam should be returned by the Muslims to the disbelievers. (That is the judgment of Allah; He judges between you. And Allah is Knowing and Wise) - This concludes by clarifying that this is the resolution of the dispute between you and the Quraysh regarding the interpretation of the Treaty of Hudaybiyyah concerning the return of women. This shows that while the Qur'an did not accept the Quraysh's demand that the Muslims were bound to return migrating women, it provided a reasonable, just, and comprehensive solution that resolved a major social dilemma of the time and prevented future disputes. Allah is All-Knowing and All-Wise, so His servants should trust His decisions.

(And if any of your wives have gone from you to the disbelievers and then you have your turn, then give to those whose wives have gone the equivalent of what they had spent. And fear Allah, in Whom you are believers) (11).
The word 'aqabtum' means 'your turn came' or 'you had the opportunity'.
(A just form of compensation): The meaning is: if a situation arises where the disbelievers violate the principle of (ask for what you have spent and let them ask for what they have spent) and refuse to return the dower paid by a Muslim husband to his wife who went to them, then the Muslims have the right to compensate him. Instead of paying the dower of a migrating woman to the disbelievers, they may give it to their Muslim brother whose departing wife's dower was not returned. This is a fair and just measure of recourse allowed because one party chose to act unjustly. (And fear Allah, in Whom you are believers) - This emphasizes caution in utilizing this permission. Even when dealing with an enemy, fear Allah in Whom you believe; do not seek excuses to take retaliatory measures without a valid reason, nor try to take more than your rightful due.

(O Prophet, when the believing women come to you pledging allegiance to you that they will not associate anything with Allah, nor will they steal, nor will they commit adultery, nor will they kill their children, nor will they bring a slander that they have fabricated between their hands and feet, nor will they disobey you in what is right—then accept their pledge and ask forgiveness for them of Allah. Indeed, Allah is Forgiving and Merciful) (12).
(A precautionary instruction regarding women in general): Just as instructions were given to examine migrating women, this verse instructs that women who come to enter Islam should not be admitted unconditionally. Instead, they must pledge to observe all the recognized virtues of Islam, and specifically promise to avoid those evils that were common in pre-Islamic jahiliyyah society, so that the seeds of these vices do not spread within the Islamic community.
It should be noted that this instruction was given at a time when, like men, crowds of women began coming to the Prophet (peace and blessings of Allah be upon him) to pledge allegiance, including many from social backgrounds where moral standards during the pre-Islamic period were very low. This situation necessitated emphasizing the avoidance of these evils, ensuring they understood the characteristics of the new society they were entering and joined with a conscious intention to reform themselves. The evils they are instructed to pledge to avoid are:
(that they will not associate anything with Allah): This means they will not associate any partner with Allah. Since the foundation of the entire religion is pure monotheism, the instruction first commands taking a pledge to avoid shirk. For an average mind, the easiest path to understanding monotheism is through the rejection of shirk. Without a conscious effort to avoid shirk, many people remain steeped in its practices while under the illusion that they practice pure monotheism. (nor will they steal): The second is that they will not steal. Just as it is unlawful to misappropriate what belongs to God, it is also unlawful to wrongfully take the property of His servants. (nor will they commit adultery): The third is that they will not commit adultery. We have pointed out the similarities between adultery and shirk in various places in this book, so there is no need to repeat them here. (nor will they kill their children): The fourth is that they will not kill their children. In the pre-Islamic period, infanticide was practiced due to superstitious beliefs, fear of poverty, or misplaced pride. In modern times, other motives have emerged for similar practices. This prohibition covers all of them. (nor will they bring a slander that they have fabricated between their hands and feet): The fifth is that they will not fabricate slander against anyone, whether man or woman, regarding what is between their hands and feet.
In my view, the phrase 'between their hands and feet' refers to the reproductive organs, which are located between the hands and feet. This is a refined and polite way of referring to them, providing a dignified cover while remaining comprehensive. It refers in a highly cultured manner to all slanders of a sexual nature, such as false accusations of adultery, illicit touching, or inappropriate behavior.
Slander of any kind is a grave sin, but slander related to sexual matters is doubly severe because it constitutes a highly damaging attack on the honor and reputation of the person accused. It can also ignite fitnah (discord) within society that becomes impossible to suppress. Slander is a sin whether committed by a man or a woman, and the Shariah penalizes both; however, women are specifically warned against it here because a woman's slander, especially of a sexual nature, is a highly destructive accusation that is exceptionally difficult to defend against. Many commentators have interpreted this phrase as meaning that a woman should not attribute another man's child to her husband, but I see no reason to restrict it so narrowly. Based on the wording, I have provided a broader interpretation that encompasses all matters that can fall under this description.
(nor will they disobey you in what is right): The sixth is that they will not disobey you in any recognized virtue (ma'ruf). 'Ma'ruf' (the right/good) is the opposite of 'munkar' (the wrong/evil), and all the specific items mentioned previously belong to the category of 'munkar'. None of the positive virtues, such as prayer, zakat, fasting, Hajj, or justice, are explicitly listed here. The reason, as indicated, is that this refers to the allegiance of women who were entering Islam in large numbers around the time of the Conquest of Mecca, among whom these pre-Islamic vices were prevalent. This specific situation required that they explicitly pledge to avoid these evils, while a comprehensive pledge was taken at the end regarding virtues, promising that they would not disobey the Prophet (peace and blessings of Allah be upon him) in any recognized virtue of the Shariah. Since the virtues of the Shariah are well known, there was no need to list them in detail here.
(A misconception): Some have taken the words 'in what is right' (fi ma'ruf) as a restriction or condition, deriving the point that even obedience to the Prophet in Islam is restricted by the condition of 'what is right', so what of others? In our view, this is an unnecessary and weak interpretation. A prophet does not and cannot command anything evil; in matters of religion, he only commands what is right, for he is under divine protection and serves as the criterion for distinguishing truth from falsehood. Therefore, obedience to his every command is an essential requirement of faith. Since he himself is the criterion for what is right, it is meaningless for anyone to judge his command as being contrary to 'what is right'. The only limitation in pledging allegiance to the Prophet is one's capability, and narrations indicate that the Prophet (peace and blessings of Allah be upon him) himself would remind people of this limit during the pledge. However, the idea that his obedience is restricted by a condition of 'what is right' is baseless. Of course, the pledge to the Caliphs and leaders after the Prophet (peace and blessings of Allah be upon him) is indeed restricted by obedience 'in what is right', as they may potentially issue a command contrary to the Shariah; thus, absolute obedience to anyone after the Prophet is not permissible for a Muslim. The manner of taking the pledge of allegiance from women is detailed in the narrations. It took various forms as described in the hadiths, but it is established that the Prophet (peace and blessings of Allah be upon him) never shook hands with women when accepting their pledge.
(then accept their pledge and ask forgiveness for them of Allah. Indeed, Allah is Forgiving and Merciful): It is commanded: Accept the pledge of those women who commit to avoiding all these evils and adhering to the virtues of the religion, and pray to Allah for their forgiveness, so that He may overlook their past mistakes. Allah is Forgiving and Merciful; He will show them mercy.

(O you who have believed, do not make allies of a people with whom Allah has become angry. They have despaired of the Hereafter just as the disbelievers despair of the inhabitants of the graves) (13).
(Repetition of the opening theme at the end): This final verse restates the theme with which the Surah began. There are many examples in the Qur'an where a Surah concludes with the same theme it started with, which is a strong proof of the Qur'an's structural coherence. The first verse states: (O you who have believed, do not take My enemies and your enemies as allies). The enemies of Allah and the believers included both the Jews and the polytheists of the Quraysh. In the Surahs preceding Surah Al-Mumtahanah, the conspiracies of the Jews and the behavior of their associates were primarily discussed, and Muslims were instructed to keep away from them. Now, in this final verse, both enemies are brought together to warn the Muslims: neither the friendship of the Jews nor that of the polytheists can bring you any good. Both are on the same level in their beliefs and actions, and will face the same end. Whoever allies with them will share their fate.
(a people with whom Allah has become angry): This clearly refers to the Jews. As an attribute, these words are used in the Qur'an specifically for the Jews; in the very first Surah, they are described as 'those who have incurred anger' (maghdubi 'alayhim). (They have despaired of the Hereafter) - That is, although they verbally profess belief in the Hereafter, their worldliness, greed for wealth, and flight from death prove that they have no real expectation of the Hereafter. If they truly expected it, they would not have committed the acts they did, nor would they have persisted in them despite Allah's severe warnings.
(just as the disbelievers despair of the inhabitants of the graves): Just as the disbelievers have no hope in the resurrection of the dead who have returned to the dust, saying: (When we have died and become dust—that is a distant return) (Qaf, 50:3), these Jews likewise have no hope in the Hereafter. Regarding the Hereafter, both are on the same level. The Qur'an frequently highlights the similarity between the Jews and the polytheists so that those Muslims who held good expectations of the Jews because they were People of the Book might correct their misunderstanding and realize that both are equally misguided. With the help of Allah, the commentary of this Surah is completed. (And praise belongs to Allah in the first and the last). Rahmanabad, March 4, 1978 / Rabi' al-Awwal 23, 1398 AH.
